message.content:
Implement the following plan:

# Plan: Streamline CLAUDE.md per Best Practices

## Context

The blog post at humanlayer.dev/blog/writing-a-good-claude-md recommends:
- Keep CLAUDE.md concise (< 300 lines, ideally < 100)
- Only include instructions that apply to **every session**
- Use progressive disclosure — point to separate docs for domain details
- Don't duplicate content that exists in other docs
- Focus on WHAT (tech stack), WHY (project purpose), HOW (commands)
- Don't use it as a linter — rely on existing patterns

Current CLAUDE.md is ~183 lines and duplicates content from ARCH.md, DESIGN.md, and API.md (database tables, key interfaces, data flow, env vars, architecture layers).

## Approach

Rewrite CLAUDE.md to ~80-90 lines. Keep universally useful content, replace domain details with pointers.

### Keep (condense where possible)
- Project Overview (lines 3-9) — essential context for every session
- Tech Stack (lines 12-17) — brief, always relevant
- Build & Test Commands (lines 19-37) — HOW, always needed
- Local Development (lines 39-63) — HOW, always needed
- Core Principles (lines 178-183) — behavioral guidance
- Workflow Orchestration (lines 132-167) — behavioral guidance

### Cut (already in referenced docs)
- Environment Variables table (lines 66-75) — duplicated in README.md
- Architecture section (lines 77-109) — duplicated in ARCH.md
- Database section (lines 111-124) — duplicated in DESIGN.md
- Key Design References section (lines 126-129) — replace with a single "Key Docs" pointer section
- Key Interfaces with file paths (lines 90-97) — duplicated in ARCH.md/DESIGN.md

### Restructure
- Add a brief "Key Docs" section pointing to ARCH.md, DESIGN.md, API.md, docs/plans/lessons.md
- Merge "Task Management" and "Workflow Orchestration" into a single concise "Workflow" section
- Keep Core Principles at the top for prominence (LLMs bias toward peripheries)

## Files Modified
- `CLAUDE.md` — rewrite

## Verification
- Read the final file, confirm < 100 lines
- Confirm all referenced docs (ARCH.md, DESIGN.md, API.md) exist
- Ensure build/test commands are still present and accurate


If you need specific details from before exiting plan mode (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /Users/pc/.claude/projects/-Users-pc-web3-ReleaseBeacon/b33d59bf-c050-49e6-b5ad-d20eb1fc3e1f.jsonl

# Plan: Streamline CLAUDE.md per Best Practices

## Context

The blog post at humanlayer.dev/blog/writing-a-good-claude-md recommends:
- Keep CLAUDE.md concise (< 300 lines, ideally < 100)
- Only include instructions that apply to **every session**
- Use progressive disclosure — point to separate docs for domain details
- Don't duplicate content that exists in other docs
- Focus on WHAT (tech stack), WHY (project purpose), HOW (commands)
- Don't use it as a linter — rely on existing patterns

Current CLAUDE.md is ~183 lines and duplicates content from ARCH.md, DESIGN.md, and API.md (database tables, key interfaces, data flow, env vars, architecture layers).

## Approach

Rewrite CLAUDE.md to ~80-90 lines. Keep universally useful content, replace domain details with pointers.

### Keep (condense where possible)
- Project Overview (lines 3-9) — essential context for every session
- Tech Stack (lines 12-17) — brief, always relevant
- Build & Test Commands (lines 19-37) — HOW, always needed
- Local Development (lines 39-63) — HOW, always needed
- Core Principles (lines 178-183) — behavioral guidance
- Workflow Orchestration (lines 132-167) — behavioral guidance

### Cut (already in referenced docs)
- Environment Variables table (lines 66-75) — duplicated in README.md
- Architecture section (lines 77-109) — duplicated in ARCH.md
- Database section (lines 111-124) — duplicated in DESIGN.md
- Key Design References section (lines 126-129) — replace with a single "Key Docs" pointer section
- Key Interfaces with file paths (lines 90-97) — duplicated in ARCH.md/DESIGN.md

### Restructure
- Add a brief "Key Docs" section pointing to ARCH.md, DESIGN.md, API.md, docs/plans/lessons.md
- Merge "Task Management" and "Workflow Orchestration" into a single concise "Workflow" section
- Keep Core Principles at the top for prominence (LLMs bias toward peripheries)

## Files Modified
- `CLAUDE.md` — rewrite

## Verification
- Read the final file, confirm < 100 lines
- Confirm all referenced docs (ARCH.md, DESIGN.md, API.md) exist
- Ensure build/test commands are still present and accurate

content.text: I'll implement the plan to streamline CLAUDE.md. Let me start by reading the current file and verifying the referenced docs exist.

1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## Project Overview
     6→
     7→**Changelogue** is an agent-driven release intelligence platform that polls upstream registries (Docker Hub, GitHub) for new releases, sends source-level notifications, and uses LLM agents (ADK-Go) to produce semantic release reports.
     8→
     9→Go module: `github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue`
    10→
    11→## Tech Stack
    12→
    13→- **Backend:** Go 1.25 — polling engine, notification routing, API server
    14→- **Database/Queue/PubSub:** PostgreSQL + [River](https://github.com/riverqueue/river) v0.31.0 for job queue (`FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`), native `LISTEN`/`NOTIFY` for real-time events
    15→- **Frontend:** Next.js (React) + Tailwind CSS — dashboard (`web/`)
    16→- **Agent Intelligence:** [Google ADK-Go](https://google.github.io/adk-go/) v0.5.0 for agent orchestration — Gemini-powered semantic release analysis
    17→- **Deployment:** Single binary — Go `//go:embed` serves Next.js static export
    18→
    19→## Build & Test Commands
    20→
    21→```bash
    22→# Build
    23→go build -o changelogue ./cmd/server
    24→
    25→# Test all packages
    26→go test ./...
    27→
    28→# Test a single package
    29→go test ./internal/ingestion/...
    30→go test -v -run TestDockerHubSource ./internal/ingestion/...
    31→
    32→# Vet
    33→go vet ./...
    34→
    35→# Integration tests (requires Docker)
    36→bash scripts/integration-test.sh
    37→```
    38→
    39→## Local Development
    40→
    41→```bash
    42→# Start Postgres (docker-compose)
    43→make up
    44→
    45→# Run server (builds + starts with NO_AUTH, requires Postgres)
    46→make run
    47→
    48→# One command: start Postgres + run server
    49→make dev
    50→
    51→# Reset database
    52→make db-reset
    53→
    54→# Frontend
    55→make frontend-install
    56→make frontend-dev
    57→
    58→# Integration tests (spins up its own Postgres on port 5433)
    59→make integration-test
    60→
    61→# Cleanup everything (binary + containers + volumes)
    62→make clean
    63→```
    64→
    65→## Environment Variables
    66→
    67→| Variable | Default | Purpose |
    68→|----------|---------|---------|
    69→| `DATABASE_URL` | `postgres://localhost:5432/changelogue?sslmode=disable` | PostgreSQL connection |
    70→| `LISTEN_ADDR` | `:8080` | HTTP server bind address |
    71→| `GOOGLE_API_KEY` | (empty) | Gemini API key for agent LLM (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=gemini`) |
    72→| `LLM_PROVIDER` | `gemini` | LLM provider: `gemini` or `openai` |
    73→| `LLM_MODEL` | `gemini-2.5-flash` (gemini) / `gpt-5.2` (openai) | Model name |
    74→| `OPENAI_API_KEY` | (empty) | OpenAI API key (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=openai`) |
    75→| `OPENAI_BASE_URL` | `https://api.openai.com/v1` | OpenAI-compatible API base URL |
    76→
    77→## Architecture
    78→
    79→Four decoupled layers communicate exclusively through PostgreSQL:
    80→
    81→1. **Ingestion Layer** — Polling workers behind `IIngestionSource` interface discover new releases from upstream registries
    82→2. **Notification Routing** — River `NotifyWorker` sends source-level notifications to subscribed channels on new releases
    83→3. **Agent Layer** — ADK-Go agent (`internal/agent/`) researches releases via project-scoped tools and produces semantic release reports
    84→4. **Routing & Output** — Notification channels (Slack, Discord, webhooks) behind `Sender` interface
    85→
    86→### Transactional Outbox Pattern
    87→
    88→The critical data integrity pattern: release insert + River job enqueue happen in the same SQL transaction (`internal/ingestion/pgstore.go`). If either fails, both rollback — zero-loss guarantee.
    89→
    90→### Key Interfaces
    91→
    92→- `IIngestionSource` (`internal/ingestion/source.go`) — Polling-based providers implement `FetchNewReleases(ctx) ([]IngestionResult, error)`
    93→- `ReleaseStore` (`internal/ingestion/store.go`) — Persistence: `IngestRelease(ctx, sourceID string, result *IngestionResult) error`
    94→- `Sender` (`internal/routing/sender.go`) — Notification channel output: `Send(ctx, channel, msg) error`
    95→- `NotifyStore` (`internal/routing/worker.go`) — Data access for the notification worker
    96→- `AgentDataStore` (`internal/agent/tools.go`) — Data access for agent tools (releases, context sources)
    97→- `OrchestratorStore` (`internal/agent/orchestrator.go`) — Full data access for agent orchestrator (project, agent runs, semantic releases)
    98→
    99→### Data Flow
   100→
   101→```
   102→IIngestionSource.FetchNewReleases() → IngestionResult
   103→    → Service.ProcessResults() processes results
   104→    → PgStore.IngestRelease() in single TX: INSERT release + River InsertTx(NotifyJobArgs)
   105→    → NotifyWorker picks up job → sends source notifications + checks agent rules
   106→    → If rules match → enqueue AgentJobArgs
   107→    → AgentWorker picks up → runs ADK-Go agent → creates semantic release
   108→    → Sends project notifications
   109→```
   110→
   111→## Database
   112→
   113→Schema lives in `internal/db/migrations.go` (idempotent, runs on startup). Current tables:
   114→- `projects` — tracked software projects (central entity)
   115→- `sources` — configured ingestion sources per project (provider, repository, poll interval)
   116→- `context_sources` — read-only references for agent research (runbooks, docs)
   117→- `releases` — source-level releases, unique on `(source_id, version)`
   118→- `semantic_releases` — AI-generated project-level reports, unique on `(project_id, version)`
   119→- `semantic_release_sources` — join table linking semantic releases to source releases
   120→- `notification_channels` — standalone channels (webhook, Slack, Discord)
   121→- `subscriptions` — source-level and project-level notification subscriptions
   122→- `agent_runs` — agent execution records scoped to a project
   123→- `api_keys` — API authentication keys
   124→- River's own tables (created by `rivermigrate`)
   125→
   126→## Key Design References
   127→
   128→- `ARCH.md` — Full architecture: system diagram, data flow lifecycle
   129→- `DESIGN.md` — Component design: database schema, agent workflow, error handling
   130→
   131→## Workflow Orchestration
   132→
   133→### 1. Plan Node Default
   134→- Enter plan mode for ANY non-trivial task (3+ steps or architectural decisions)
   135→- If something goes sideways, STOP and re-plan immediately — don't keep pushing
   136→- Use plan mode for verification steps, not just building
   137→- Write detailed specs upfront to reduce ambiguity
   138→
   139→### 2. Subagent Strategy
   140→- Use subagents liberally to keep main context window clean
   141→- Offload research, exploration, and parallel analysis to subagents
   142→- For complex problems, throw more compute at it via subagents
   143→- One task per subagent for focused execution
   144→
   145→### 3. Self-Improvement Loop
   146→- After ANY correction from the user: update `docs/plans/lessons.md` with the pattern
   147→- Write rules for yourself that prevent the same mistake
   148→- Ruthlessly iterate on these lessons until mistake rate drops
   149→- Review lessons at session start for relevant project
   150→
   151→### 4. Verification Before Done
   152→- Never mark a task complete without proving it works
   153→- Diff behavior between main and your changes when relevant
   154→- Ask yourself: "Would a staff engineer approve this?"
   155→- Run tests, check logs, demonstrate correctness
   156→
   157→### 5. Demand Elegance (Balanced)
   158→- For non-trivial changes: pause and ask "is there a more elegant way?"
   159→- If a fix feels hacky: "Knowing everything I know now, implement the elegant solution"
   160→- Skip this for simple, obvious fixes — don't over-engineer
   161→- Challenge your own work before presenting it
   162→
   163→### 6. Autonomous Bug Fixing
   164→- When given a bug report: just fix it. Don't ask for hand-holding
   165→- Point at logs, errors, failing tests — then resolve them
   166→- Zero context switching required from the user
   167→- Go fix failing CI tests without being told how
   168→
   169→## Task Management
   170→
   171→1. **Plan First**: Write plan to `docs/plans/todo.md` with checkable items
   172→2. **Verify Plan**: Check in before starting implementation
   173→3. **Track Progress**: Mark items complete as you go
   174→4. **Explain Changes**: High-level summary at each step
   175→5. **Document Results**: Add review section to `docs/plans/todo.md`
   176→6. **Capture Lessons**: Update `docs/plans/lessons.md` after corrections
   177→
   178→## Core Principles
   179→
   180→- **Simplicity First**: Make every change as simple as possible. Impact minimal code.
   181→- **No Laziness**: Find root causes. No temporary fixes. Senior developer standards.
   182→- **Minimal Impact**: Changes should only touch what's necessary. Avoid introducing bugs.
   183→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

file.content:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Project Overview

**Changelogue** is an agent-driven release intelligence platform that polls upstream registries (Docker Hub, GitHub) for new releases, sends source-level notifications, and uses LLM agents (ADK-Go) to produce semantic release reports.

Go module: `github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue`

## Tech Stack

- **Backend:** Go 1.25 — polling engine, notification routing, API server
- **Database/Queue/PubSub:** PostgreSQL + [River](https://github.com/riverqueue/river) v0.31.0 for job queue (`FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`), native `LISTEN`/`NOTIFY` for real-time events
- **Frontend:** Next.js (React) + Tailwind CSS — dashboard (`web/`)
- **Agent Intelligence:** [Google ADK-Go](https://google.github.io/adk-go/) v0.5.0 for agent orchestration — Gemini-powered semantic release analysis
- **Deployment:** Single binary — Go `//go:embed` serves Next.js static export

## Build & Test Commands

```bash
# Build
go build -o changelogue ./cmd/server

# Test all packages
go test ./...

# Test a single package
go test ./internal/ingestion/...
go test -v -run TestDockerHubSource ./internal/ingestion/...

# Vet
go vet ./...

# Integration tests (requires Docker)
bash scripts/integration-test.sh
```

## Local Development

```bash
# Start Postgres (docker-compose)
make up

# Run server (builds + starts with NO_AUTH, requires Postgres)
make run

# One command: start Postgres + run server
make dev

# Reset database
make db-reset

# Frontend
make frontend-install
make frontend-dev

# Integration tests (spins up its own Postgres on port 5433)
make integration-test

# Cleanup everything (binary + containers + volumes)
make clean
```

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `DATABASE_URL` | `postgres://localhost:5432/changelogue?sslmode=disable` | PostgreSQL connection |
| `LISTEN_ADDR` | `:8080` | HTTP server bind address |
| `GOOGLE_API_KEY` | (empty) | Gemini API key for agent LLM (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=gemini`) |
| `LLM_PROVIDER` | `gemini` | LLM provider: `gemini` or `openai` |
| `LLM_MODEL` | `gemini-2.5-flash` (gemini) / `gpt-5.2` (openai) | Model name |
| `OPENAI_API_KEY` | (empty) | OpenAI API key (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=openai`) |
| `OPENAI_BASE_URL` | `https://api.openai.com/v1` | OpenAI-compatible API base URL |

## Architecture

Four decoupled layers communicate exclusively through PostgreSQL:

1. **Ingestion Layer** — Polling workers behind `IIngestionSource` interface discover new releases from upstream registries
2. **Notification Routing** — River `NotifyWorker` sends source-level notifications to subscribed channels on new releases
3. **Agent Layer** — ADK-Go agent (`internal/agent/`) researches releases via project-scoped tools and produces semantic release reports
4. **Routing & Output** — Notification channels (Slack, Discord, webhooks) behind `Sender` interface

### Transactional Outbox Pattern

The critical data integrity pattern: release insert + River job enqueue happen in the same SQL transaction (`internal/ingestion/pgstore.go`). If either fails, both rollback — zero-loss guarantee.

### Key Interfaces

- `IIngestionSource` (`internal/ingestion/source.go`) — Polling-based providers implement `FetchNewReleases(ctx) ([]IngestionResult, error)`
- `ReleaseStore` (`internal/ingestion/store.go`) — Persistence: `IngestRelease(ctx, sourceID string, result *IngestionResult) error`
- `Sender` (`internal/routing/sender.go`) — Notification channel output: `Send(ctx, channel, msg) error`
- `NotifyStore` (`internal/routing/worker.go`) — Data access for the notification worker
- `AgentDataStore` (`internal/agent/tools.go`) — Data access for agent tools (releases, context sources)
- `OrchestratorStore` (`internal/agent/orchestrator.go`) — Full data access for agent orchestrator (project, agent runs, semantic releases)

### Data Flow

```
IIngestionSource.FetchNewReleases() → IngestionResult
    → Service.ProcessResults() processes results
    → PgStore.IngestRelease() in single TX: INSERT release + River InsertTx(NotifyJobArgs)
    → NotifyWorker picks up job → sends source notifications + checks agent rules
    → If rules match → enqueue AgentJobArgs
    → AgentWorker picks up → runs ADK-Go agent → creates semantic release
    → Sends project notifications
```

## Database

Schema lives in `internal/db/migrations.go` (idempotent, runs on startup). Current tables:
- `projects` — tracked software projects (central entity)
- `sources` — configured ingestion sources per project (provider, repository, poll interval)
- `context_sources` — read-only references for agent research (runbooks, docs)
- `releases` — source-level releases, unique on `(source_id, version)`
- `semantic_releases` — AI-generated project-level reports, unique on `(project_id, version)`
- `semantic_release_sources` — join table linking semantic releases to source releases
- `notification_channels` — standalone channels (webhook, Slack, Discord)
- `subscriptions` — source-level and project-level notification subscriptions
- `agent_runs` — agent execution records scoped to a project
- `api_keys` — API authentication keys
- River's own tables (created by `rivermigrate`)

## Key Design References

- `ARCH.md` — Full architecture: system diagram, data flow lifecycle
- `DESIGN.md` — Component design: database schema, agent workflow, error handling

## Workflow Orchestration

### 1. Plan Node Default
- Enter plan mode for ANY non-trivial task (3+ steps or architectural decisions)
- If something goes sideways, STOP and re-plan immediately — don't keep pushing
- Use plan mode for verification steps, not just building
- Write detailed specs upfront to reduce ambiguity

### 2. Subagent Strategy
- Use subagents liberally to keep main context window clean
- Offload research, exploration, and parallel analysis to subagents
- For complex problems, throw more compute at it via subagents
- One task per subagent for focused execution

### 3. Self-Improvement Loop
- After ANY correction from the user: update `docs/plans/lessons.md` with the pattern
- Write rules for yourself that prevent the same mistake
- Ruthlessly iterate on these lessons until mistake rate drops
- Review lessons at session start for relevant project

### 4. Verification Before Done
- Never mark a task complete without proving it works
- Diff behavior between main and your changes when relevant
- Ask yourself: "Would a staff engineer approve this?"
- Run tests, check logs, demonstrate correctness

### 5. Demand Elegance (Balanced)
- For non-trivial changes: pause and ask "is there a more elegant way?"
- If a fix feels hacky: "Knowing everything I know now, implement the elegant solution"
- Skip this for simple, obvious fixes — don't over-engineer
- Challenge your own work before presenting it

### 6. Autonomous Bug Fixing
- When given a bug report: just fix it. Don't ask for hand-holding
- Point at logs, errors, failing tests — then resolve them
- Zero context switching required from the user
- Go fix failing CI tests without being told how

## Task Management

1. **Plan First**: Write plan to `docs/plans/todo.md` with checkable items
2. **Verify Plan**: Check in before starting implementation
3. **Track Progress**: Mark items complete as you go
4. **Explain Changes**: High-level summary at each step
5. **Document Results**: Add review section to `docs/plans/todo.md`
6. **Capture Lessons**: Update `docs/plans/lessons.md` after corrections

## Core Principles

- **Simplicity First**: Make every change as simple as possible. Impact minimal code.
- **No Laziness**: Find root causes. No temporary fixes. Senior developer standards.
- **Minimal Impact**: Changes should only touch what's necessary. Avoid introducing bugs.

content.text: All referenced docs exist. Now I'll rewrite CLAUDE.md following the plan — condensing to ~80-90 lines, cutting duplicated content, and restructuring.

input.content:
# CLAUDE.md

## Core Principles

- **Simplicity First**: Make every change as simple as possible. Impact minimal code.
- **No Laziness**: Find root causes. No temporary fixes. Senior developer standards.
- **Minimal Impact**: Changes should only touch what's necessary. Avoid introducing bugs.

## Project Overview

**Changelogue** is an agent-driven release intelligence platform that polls upstream registries (Docker Hub, GitHub) for new releases, sends source-level notifications, and uses LLM agents (ADK-Go) to produce semantic release reports.

Go module: `github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue`

## Tech Stack

- **Backend:** Go 1.25 — polling engine, notification routing, API server
- **Database/Queue/PubSub:** PostgreSQL + [River](https://github.com/riverqueue/river) v0.31.0 for job queue, native `LISTEN`/`NOTIFY` for real-time events
- **Frontend:** Next.js (React) + Tailwind CSS — dashboard (`web/`)
- **Agent Intelligence:** [Google ADK-Go](https://google.github.io/adk-go/) v0.5.0 — Gemini-powered semantic release analysis
- **Deployment:** Single binary — Go `//go:embed` serves Next.js static export

## Build & Test Commands

```bash
# Build
go build -o changelogue ./cmd/server

# Test all packages
go test ./...

# Test a single package
go test ./internal/ingestion/...
go test -v -run TestDockerHubSource ./internal/ingestion/...

# Vet
go vet ./...

# Integration tests (requires Docker)
bash scripts/integration-test.sh
```

## Local Development

```bash
make up                  # Start Postgres (docker-compose)
make run                 # Run server (builds + starts with NO_AUTH, requires Postgres)
make dev                 # One command: start Postgres + run server
make db-reset            # Reset database
make frontend-install    # Install frontend deps
make frontend-dev        # Run frontend dev server
make integration-test    # Integration tests (own Postgres on port 5433)
make clean               # Cleanup everything (binary + containers + volumes)
```

## Key Docs

- `ARCH.md` — Architecture, data flow, key interfaces, system diagram
- `DESIGN.md` — Database schema, agent workflow, error handling
- `API.md` — REST API endpoints and examples
- `README.md` — Environment variables, deployment, setup
- `docs/plans/lessons.md` — Accumulated lessons from past corrections

## Workflow

### Planning & Execution
- Enter plan mode for ANY non-trivial task (3+ steps or architectural decisions)
- Write plan to `docs/plans/todo.md` with checkable items; check in before starting
- If something goes sideways, STOP and re-plan immediately
- Mark items complete as you go; add review section when done

### Subagents
- Use subagents liberally to keep main context window clean
- Offload research, exploration, and parallel analysis to subagents
- One task per subagent for focused execution

### Verification
- Never mark a task complete without proving it works
- Run tests, check logs, demonstrate correctness
- Ask yourself: "Would a staff engineer approve this?"

### Self-Improvement
- After ANY correction: update `docs/plans/lessons.md` with the pattern
- Review lessons at session start

### Bug Fixing
- When given a bug report: just fix it autonomously
- Point at logs, errors, failing tests — then resolve them

content.content: The file /Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/CLAUDE.md has been updated successfully.

## Core Principles

- **Simplicity First**: Make every change as simple as possible. Impact minimal code.
- **No Laziness**: Find root causes. No temporary fixes. Senior developer standards.

## Project Overview

## Key Docs

- `API.md` — REST API endpoints and examples
- `README.md` — Environment variables, deployment, setup
- `docs/plans/lessons.md` — Accumulated lessons from past corrections

## Workflow

### Planning & Execution
- Enter plan mode for ANY non-trivial task (3+ steps or architectural decisions)
- Write plan to `docs/plans/todo.md` with checkable items; check in before starting
- If something goes sideways, STOP and re-plan immediately
- Mark items complete as you go; add review section when done

### Subagents
- Use subagents liberally to keep main context window clean
- Offload research, exploration, and parallel analysis to subagents
- One task per subagent for focused execution

### Verification
- Never mark a task complete without proving it works
- Run tests, check logs, demonstrate correctness
- Ask yourself: "Would a staff engineer approve this?"

### Self-Improvement
- After ANY correction: update `docs/plans/lessons.md` with the pattern
- Review lessons at session start

### Bug Fixing
- When given a bug report: just fix it autonomously
- Point at logs, errors, failing tests — then resolve them

-This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
+## Core Principles
+- **Simplicity First**: Make every change as simple as possible. Impact minimal code.
+- **No Laziness**: Find root causes. No temporary fixes. Senior developer standards.
+- **Minimal Impact**: Changes should only touch what's necessary. Avoid introducing bugs.
## Project Overview

**Changelogue** is an agent-driven release intelligence platform that polls upstream registries (Docker Hub, GitHub) for new releases, sends source-level notifications, and uses LLM agents (ADK-Go) to produce semantic release reports.
## Tech Stack
- **Backend:** Go 1.25 — polling engine, notification routing, API server
-- **Database/Queue/PubSub:** PostgreSQL + [River](https://github.com/riverqueue/river) v0.31.0 for job queue (`FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`), native `LISTEN`/`NOTIFY` for real-time events
+- **Database/Queue/PubSub:** PostgreSQL + [River](https://github.com/riverqueue/river) v0.31.0 for job queue, native `LISTEN`/`NOTIFY` for real-time events
- **Frontend:** Next.js (React) + Tailwind CSS — dashboard (`web/`)
-- **Agent Intelligence:** [Google ADK-Go](https://google.github.io/adk-go/) v0.5.0 for agent orchestration — Gemini-powered semantic release analysis
+- **Agent Intelligence:** [Google ADK-Go](https://google.github.io/adk-go/) v0.5.0 — Gemini-powered semantic release analysis
- **Deployment:** Single binary — Go `//go:embed` serves Next.js static export
## Build & Test Commands
## Local Development

-## Architecture
+## Workflow
-Four decoupled layers communicate exclusively through PostgreSQL:
-1. **Ingestion Layer** — Polling workers behind `IIngestionSource` interface discover new releases from upstream registries
-2. **Notification Routing** — River `NotifyWorker` sends source-level notifications to subscribed channels on new releases
-3. **Agent Layer** — ADK-Go agent (`internal/agent/`) researches releases via project-scoped tools and produces semantic release reports
-4. **Routing & Output** — Notification channels (Slack, Discord, webhooks) behind `Sender` interface

-The critical data integrity pattern: release insert + River job enqueue happen in the same SQL transaction (`internal/ingestion/pgstore.go`). If either fails, both rollback — zero-loss guarantee.
-### Key Interfaces
-- `IIngestionSource` (`internal/ingestion/source.go`) — Polling-based providers implement `FetchNewReleases(ctx) ([]IngestionResult, error)`
-- `ReleaseStore` (`internal/ingestion/store.go`) — Persistence: `IngestRelease(ctx, sourceID string, result *IngestionResult) error`
-- `Sender` (`internal/routing/sender.go`) — Notification channel output: `Send(ctx, channel, msg) error`
-- `NotifyStore` (`internal/routing/worker.go`) — Data access for the notification worker
-- `AgentDataStore` (`internal/agent/tools.go`) — Data access for agent tools (releases, context sources)
-- `OrchestratorStore` (`internal/agent/orchestrator.go`) — Full data access for agent orchestrator (project, agent runs, semantic releases)
-### Data Flow
-IIngestionSource.FetchNewReleases() → IngestionResult
-    → Service.ProcessResults() processes results
-    → PgStore.IngestRelease() in single TX: INSERT release + River InsertTx(NotifyJobArgs)
-    → NotifyWorker picks up job → sends source notifications + checks agent rules
-    → If rules match → enqueue AgentJobArgs
-    → AgentWorker picks up → runs ADK-Go agent → creates semantic release
-    → Sends project notifications
-## Database
-Schema lives in `internal/db/migrations.go` (idempotent, runs on startup). Current tables:
-- `projects` — tracked software projects (central entity)
-- `sources` — configured ingestion sources per project (provider, repository, poll interval)
-- `context_sources` — read-only references for agent research (runbooks, docs)
-- `releases` — source-level releases, unique on `(source_id, version)`
-- `semantic_releases` — AI-generated project-level reports, unique on `(project_id, version)`
-- `semantic_release_sources` — join table linking semantic releases to source releases
-- `notification_channels` — standalone channels (webhook, Slack, Discord)
-- `subscriptions` — source-level and project-level notification subscriptions
-- `agent_runs` — agent execution records scoped to a project
-- `api_keys` — API authentication keys
-- River's own tables (created by `rivermigrate`)
-## Key Design References
-- `ARCH.md` — Full architecture: system diagram, data flow lifecycle
-- `DESIGN.md` — Component design: database schema, agent workflow, error handling
-## Workflow Orchestration
-### 1. Plan Node Default
+### Planning & Execution
- Enter plan mode for ANY non-trivial task (3+ steps or architectural decisions)
-- If something goes sideways, STOP and re-plan immediately — don't keep pushing
-- Use plan mode for verification steps, not just building

- Run tests, check logs, demonstrate correctness

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Project Overview

**Changelogue** is an agent-driven release intelligence platform that polls upstream registries (Docker Hub, GitHub) for new releases, sends source-level notifications, and uses LLM agents (ADK-Go) to produce semantic release reports.

Go module: `github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue`

## Tech Stack

- **Backend:** Go 1.25 — polling engine, notification routing, API server
- **Database/Queue/PubSub:** PostgreSQL + [River](https://github.com/riverqueue/river) v0.31.0 for job queue (`FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`), native `LISTEN`/`NOTIFY` for real-time events
- **Frontend:** Next.js (React) + Tailwind CSS — dashboard (`web/`)
- **Agent Intelligence:** [Google ADK-Go](https://google.github.io/adk-go/) v0.5.0 for agent orchestration — Gemini-powered semantic release analysis
- **Deployment:** Single binary — Go `//go:embed` serves Next.js static export

## Build & Test Commands

```bash
# Build
go build -o changelogue ./cmd/server

# Test all packages
go test ./...

# Test a single package
go test ./internal/ingestion/...
go test -v -run TestDockerHubSource ./internal/ingestion/...

# Vet
go vet ./...

# Integration tests (requires Docker)
bash scripts/integration-test.sh
```

## Local Development

```bash
# Start Postgres (docker-compose)
make up

# Run server (builds + starts with NO_AUTH, requires Postgres)
make run

# One command: start Postgres + run server
make dev

# Reset database
make db-reset

# Frontend
make frontend-install
make frontend-dev

# Integration tests (spins up its own Postgres on port 5433)
make integration-test

# Cleanup everything (binary + containers + volumes)
make clean
```

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `DATABASE_URL` | `postgres://localhost:5432/changelogue?sslmode=disable` | PostgreSQL connection |
| `LISTEN_ADDR` | `:8080` | HTTP server bind address |
| `GOOGLE_API_KEY` | (empty) | Gemini API key for agent LLM (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=gemini`) |
| `LLM_PROVIDER` | `gemini` | LLM provider: `gemini` or `openai` |
| `LLM_MODEL` | `gemini-2.5-flash` (gemini) / `gpt-5.2` (openai) | Model name |
| `OPENAI_API_KEY` | (empty) | OpenAI API key (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=openai`) |
| `OPENAI_BASE_URL` | `https://api.openai.com/v1` | OpenAI-compatible API base URL |

## Architecture

Four decoupled layers communicate exclusively through PostgreSQL:

1. **Ingestion Layer** — Polling workers behind `IIngestionSource` interface discover new releases from upstream registries
2. **Notification Routing** — River `NotifyWorker` sends source-level notifications to subscribed channels on new releases
3. **Agent Layer** — ADK-Go agent (`internal/agent/`) researches releases via project-scoped tools and produces semantic release reports
4. **Routing & Output** — Notification channels (Slack, Discord, webhooks) behind `Sender` interface

### Transactional Outbox Pattern

The critical data integrity pattern: release insert + River job enqueue happen in the same SQL transaction (`internal/ingestion/pgstore.go`). If either fails, both rollback — zero-loss guarantee.

### Key Interfaces

- `IIngestionSource` (`internal/ingestion/source.go`) — Polling-based providers implement `FetchNewReleases(ctx) ([]IngestionResult, error)`
- `ReleaseStore` (`internal/ingestion/store.go`) — Persistence: `IngestRelease(ctx, sourceID string, result *IngestionResult) error`
- `Sender` (`internal/routing/sender.go`) — Notification channel output: `Send(ctx, channel, msg) error`
- `NotifyStore` (`internal/routing/worker.go`) — Data access for the notification worker
- `AgentDataStore` (`internal/agent/tools.go`) — Data access for agent tools (releases, context sources)
- `OrchestratorStore` (`internal/agent/orchestrator.go`) — Full data access for agent orchestrator (project, agent runs, semantic releases)

### Data Flow

```
IIngestionSource.FetchNewReleases() → IngestionResult
    → Service.ProcessResults() processes results
    → PgStore.IngestRelease() in single TX: INSERT release + River InsertTx(NotifyJobArgs)
    → NotifyWorker picks up job → sends source notifications + checks agent rules
    → If rules match → enqueue AgentJobArgs
    → AgentWorker picks up → runs ADK-Go agent → creates semantic release
    → Sends project notifications
```

## Database

Schema lives in `internal/db/migrations.go` (idempotent, runs on startup). Current tables:
- `projects` — tracked software projects (central entity)
- `sources` — configured ingestion sources per project (provider, repository, poll interval)
- `context_sources` — read-only references for agent research (runbooks, docs)
- `releases` — source-level releases, unique on `(source_id, version)`
- `semantic_releases` — AI-generated project-level reports, unique on `(project_id, version)`
- `semantic_release_sources` — join table linking semantic releases to source releases
- `notification_channels` — standalone channels (webhook, Slack, Discord)
- `subscriptions` — source-level and project-level notification subscriptions
- `agent_runs` — agent execution records scoped to a project
- `api_keys` — API authentication keys
- River's own tables (created by `rivermigrate`)

## Key Design References

- `ARCH.md` — Full architecture: system diagram, data flow lifecycle
- `DESIGN.md` — Component design: database schema, agent workflow, error handling

## Workflow Orchestration

### 1. Plan Node Default
- Enter plan mode for ANY non-trivial task (3+ steps or architectural decisions)
- If something goes sideways, STOP and re-plan immediately — don't keep pushing
- Use plan mode for verification steps, not just building
- Write detailed specs upfront to reduce ambiguity

### 2. Subagent Strategy
- Use subagents liberally to keep main context window clean
- Offload research, exploration, and parallel analysis to subagents
- For complex problems, throw more compute at it via subagents
- One task per subagent for focused execution

### 3. Self-Improvement Loop
- After ANY correction from the user: update `docs/plans/lessons.md` with the pattern
- Write rules for yourself that prevent the same mistake
- Ruthlessly iterate on these lessons until mistake rate drops
- Review lessons at session start for relevant project

### 4. Verification Before Done
- Never mark a task complete without proving it works
- Diff behavior between main and your changes when relevant
- Ask yourself: "Would a staff engineer approve this?"
- Run tests, check logs, demonstrate correctness

### 5. Demand Elegance (Balanced)
- For non-trivial changes: pause and ask "is there a more elegant way?"
- If a fix feels hacky: "Knowing everything I know now, implement the elegant solution"
- Skip this for simple, obvious fixes — don't over-engineer
- Challenge your own work before presenting it

### 6. Autonomous Bug Fixing
- When given a bug report: just fix it. Don't ask for hand-holding
- Point at logs, errors, failing tests — then resolve them
- Zero context switching required from the user
- Go fix failing CI tests without being told how

## Task Management

1. **Plan First**: Write plan to `docs/plans/todo.md` with checkable items
2. **Verify Plan**: Check in before starting implementation
3. **Track Progress**: Mark items complete as you go
4. **Explain Changes**: High-level summary at each step
5. **Document Results**: Add review section to `docs/plans/todo.md`
6. **Capture Lessons**: Update `docs/plans/lessons.md` after corrections

## Core Principles

- **Simplicity First**: Make every change as simple as possible. Impact minimal code.
- **No Laziness**: Find root causes. No temporary fixes. Senior developer standards.
- **Minimal Impact**: Changes should only touch what's necessary. Avoid introducing bugs.

content.text: Let me verify the line count.

88 lines — right in the target range. Here's a summary of changes:

**Removed (duplicated elsewhere):**
- Environment Variables table → already in README.md
- Architecture section (4 layers, transactional outbox, data flow diagram) → already in ARCH.md
- Key Interfaces with file paths → already in ARCH.md/DESIGN.md
- Database tables listing → already in DESIGN.md
- Key Design References section → replaced by new "Key Docs" section

**Restructured:**
- Moved Core Principles to the top for prominence (LLMs bias toward beginning/end)
- Merged "Task Management" and "Workflow Orchestration" into a single "Workflow" section with concise subsections
- Added "Key Docs" section pointing to ARCH.md, DESIGN.md, API.md, README.md, and docs/plans/lessons.md
- Condensed Local Development from verbose comments to a compact table-style format

**Result:** 183 lines → 88 lines (52% reduction), no loss of universally-needed information.

 ## Local Development

 ```bash
+make clean               # Cleanup everything (binary + containers + volumes)
 ```
 
-## Environment Variables
-
-| Variable | Default | Purpose |
-|----------|---------|---------|
-| `DATABASE_URL` | `postgres://localhost:5432/changelogue?sslmode=disable` | PostgreSQL connection |
-| `LISTEN_ADDR` | `:8080` | HTTP server bind address |
-| `GOOGLE_API_KEY` | (empty) | Gemini API key for agent LLM (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=gemini`) |
-| `LLM_PROVIDER` | `gemini` | LLM provider: `gemini` or `openai` |
-| `LLM_MODEL` | `gemini-2.5-flash` (gemini) / `gpt-5.2` (openai) | Model name |
-| `OPENAI_API_KEY` | (empty) | OpenAI API key (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=openai`) |
-| `OPENAI_BASE_URL` | `https://api.openai.com/v1` | OpenAI-compatible API base URL |
-
-## Architecture
-
-Four decoupled layers communicate exclusively through PostgreSQL:
-
-1. **Ingestion Layer** — Polling workers behind `IIngestionSource` interface discover new releases from upstream registries
-2. **Notification Routing** — River `NotifyWorker` sends source-level notifications to subscribed channels on new releases
-3. **Agent Layer** — ADK-Go agent (`internal/agent/`) researches releases via project-scoped tools and produces semantic release reports
-4. **Routing & Output** — Notification channels (Slack, Discord, webhooks) behind `Sender` interface
+## Key Docs
 
-### Transactional Outbox Pattern
+- `ARCH.md` — Architecture, data flow, key interfaces, system diagram
+- `DESIGN.md` — Database schema, agent workflow, error handling
+- `API.md` — REST API endpoints and examples
+- `README.md` — Environment variables, deployment, setup
+- `docs/plans/lessons.md` — Accumulated lessons from past corrections
 
-The critical data integrity pattern: release insert + River job enqueue happen in the same SQL transaction (`internal/ingestion/pgstore.go`). If either fails, both rollback — zero-loss guarantee.
+## Workflow
 
-### Key Interfaces
-
-- `IIngestionSource` (`internal/ingestion/source.go`) — Polling-based providers implement `FetchNewReleases(ctx) ([]IngestionResult, error)`
-- `ReleaseStore` (`internal/ingestion/store.go`) — Persistence: `IngestRelease(ctx, sourceID string, result *IngestionResult) error`
-- `Sender` (`internal/routing/sender.go`) — Notification channel output: `Send(ctx, channel, msg) error`
-- `NotifyStore` (`internal/routing/worker.go`) — Data access for the notification worker
-- `AgentDataStore` (`internal/agent/tools.go`) — Data access for agent tools (releases, context sources)
-- `OrchestratorStore` (`internal/agent/orchestrator.go`) — Full data access for agent orchestrator (project, agent runs, semantic releases)
-
-### Data Flow
-
-```
-IIngestionSource.FetchNewReleases() → IngestionResult
-    → Service.ProcessResults() processes results
-    → PgStore.IngestRelease() in single TX: INSERT release + River InsertTx(NotifyJobArgs)
-    → NotifyWorker picks up job → sends source notifications + checks agent rules
-    → If rules match → enqueue AgentJobArgs
-    → AgentWorker picks up → runs ADK-Go agent → creates semantic release
-    → Sends project notifications
-```
-
-## Database
-
-Schema lives in `internal/db/migrations.go` (idempotent, runs on startup). Current tables:
-- `projects` — tracked software projects (central entity)
-- `sources` — configured ingestion sources per project (provider, repository, poll interval)
-- `context_sources` — read-only references for agent research (runbooks, docs)
-- `releases` — source-level releases, unique on `(source_id, version)`
-- `semantic_releases` — AI-generated project-level reports, unique on `(project_id, version)`
-- `semantic_release_sources` — join table linking semantic releases to source releases
-- `notification_channels` — standalone channels (webhook, Slack, Discord)
-- `subscriptions` — source-level and project-level notification subscriptions
-- `agent_runs` — agent execution records scoped to a project
-- `api_keys` — API authentication keys
-- River's own tables (created by `rivermigrate`)
-
-## Key Design References
-
-- `ARCH.md` — Full architecture: system diagram, data flow lifecycle
-- `DESIGN.md` — Component design: database schema, agent workflow, error handling
-
-## Workflow Orchestration
-
-### 1. Plan Node Default
+### Planning & Execution
 - Enter plan mode for ANY non-trivial task (3+ steps or architectural decisions)
-- If something goes sideways, STOP and re-plan immediately — don't keep pushing
-- Use plan mode for verification steps, not just building
-- Write detailed specs upfront to reduce ambiguity
+- Write plan to `docs/plans/todo.md` with checkable items; check in before starting
+- If something goes sideways, STOP and re-plan immediately
+- Mark items complete as you go; add review section when done
 
-### 2. Subagent Strategy
+### Subagents
 - Use subagents liberally to keep main context window clean
 - Offload research, exploration, and parallel analysis to subagents
-- For complex problems, throw more compute at it via subagents
 - One task per subagent for focused execution
 
-### 3. Self-Improvement Loop
-- After ANY correction from the user: update `docs/plans/lessons.md` with the pattern
-- Write rules for yourself that prevent the same mistake
-- Ruthlessly iterate on these lessons until mistake rate drops
-- Review lessons at session start for relevant project
-
-### 4. Verification Before Done
+### Verification
 - Never mark a task complete without proving it works
-- Diff behavior between main and your changes when relevant
-- Ask yourself: "Would a staff engineer approve this?"
 - Run tests, check logs, demonstrate correctness
+- Ask yourself: "Would a staff engineer approve this?"
 
-### 5. Demand Elegance (Balanced)
-- For non-trivial changes: pause and ask "is there a more elegant way?"
-- If a fix feels hacky: "Knowing everything I know now, implement the elegant solution"
-- Skip this for simple, obvious fixes — don't over-engineer
-- Challenge your own work before presenting it
+### Self-Improvement
+- After ANY correction: update `docs/plans/lessons.md` with the pattern
+- Review lessons at session start
 
-### 6. Autonomous Bug Fixing
-- When given a bug report: just fix it. Don't ask for hand-holding
+### Bug Fixing
+- When given a bug report: just fix it autonomously
 - Point at logs, errors, failing tests — then resolve them
-- Zero context switching required from the user
-- Go fix failing CI tests without being told how
-
-## Task Management
-
-1. **Plan First**: Write plan to `docs/plans/todo.md` with checkable items
-2. **Verify Plan**: Check in before starting implementation
-3. **Track Progress**: Mark items complete as you go
-4. **Explain Changes**: High-level summary at each step
-5. **Document Results**: Add review section to `docs/plans/todo.md`
-6. **Capture Lessons**: Update `docs/plans/lessons.md` after corrections
-
-## Core Principles
-
-- **Simplicity First**: Make every change as simple as possible. Impact minimal code.
-- **No Laziness**: Find root causes. No temporary fixes. Senior developer standards.
-- **Minimal Impact**: Changes should only touch what's necessary. Avoid introducing bugs.
